analogy: Think of "Principles of Inheritance" through the chapter's own opening images: CHAPTER 5 MOLECULAR BASIS OF INHERITANCE 5.1 The DNA 5.2 The Search for Genetic Material 5.3 RNA World 5.4 Replication 5.5 Transcription 5.6 Genetic Code 5.7 Translation 5.8 Regulation of Gene Expression 5.9 Human Genome Project 5.10 DNA Fingerprinting In the previous chapter, you have... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Principles of Inheritance is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that cHAPTER 5 MOLECULAR BASIS OF INHERITANCE 5.1 The DNA 5.2 The Search for Genetic Material 5.3 RNA World 5.4 Replication 5.5 Transcription 5.6 Genetic Code 5.7 Translation 5.8 Regulation of Gene Expression 5.9 Human Genome Project 5.10 DNA Fingerprinting In the previous chapter, you have learnt the inheritance patterns and the genetic basis of such patterns.

The text explains that at the time of Mendel, the nature of those ‘factors’ regulating the pattern of inheritance was not clear.

The text explains that over the next hundred years, the nature of the putative genetic material was investigated culminating in the realisation that DNA – deoxyribonucleic acid – is the genetic material, at least for the majority of organisms.

The text explains that in class XI you have learnt that nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides.

The text explains that deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA) are the two types of nucleic acids found in living systems.

The text explains that dNA acts as the genetic material in most of the organisms.

The text explains that rNA though it also acts as a genetic material in some viruses, mostly functions as a messenger.

The text explains that it functions as adapter, structural, and in some cases as a catalytic molecule.

The text explains that in Class XI you have already learnt the structures of nucleotides and the way these monomer units are linked to form nucleic acid polymers.

The text explains that in this chapter we are going to discuss the structure of DNA, its replication, the process of making RNA from DNA (transcription), the genetic code that determines the sequences of amino acids in proteins, the process of protein synthesis (translation) and elementary basis of their regulation.

The text explains that 80 BIOLOGY of complete nucleotide sequence of human genome during last decade has set in a new era of genomics.

The text explains that in the last section, the essentials of human genome sequencing and its consequences will also be discussed.

The text explains that let us begin our discussion by first understanding the structure of the most interesting molecule in the living system, that is, the DNA.

The text explains that in subsequent sections, we will understand that why it is the most abundant genetic material, and what its relationship is with RNA.

The text explains that 5.1 THE DNA DNA is a long polymer of deoxyribonucleotides.

The text explains that the length of DNA is usually defined as number of nucleotides (or a pair of nucleotide referred to as base pairs) present in it.

The text explains that 83 MOLECULAR BASIS OF INHERITANCE Figure 5.4a Nucleosome Figure 5.4b EM picture - ‘Beads-on-String’ In some viruses the flow of information is in reverse direction, that is, from RNA to DNA.

The text explains that if one examines each requirement one by one, because of rule of base pairing and complementarity, both the nucleic acids (DNA and RNA) have the ability to direct their duplications.

The text explains that rNA can directly code for the synthesis of proteins, hence can easily express the characters.

The text explains that matthew Meselson and Franklin Stahl performed the following experiment in 1958: (i) They grew E. coli in a medium containing 15NH4Cl (15N is the heavy isotope of nitrogen) as the only nitrogen source for many generations.

The text explains that this heavy DNA molecule could be distinguished from the normal DNA by centrifugation in a cesium chloride (CsCl) density gradient (Please note that 15N is not a radioactive isotope, and it can be separated from 14N only based on densities).

The text explains that can you recall what centrifugal force is, and think why a molecule with higher mass/density would sediment faster?

The text explains that (iii) Thus, the DNA that was extracted from the culture one generation after the transfer from 15N to 14N medium [that is after 20 minutes; E. coli divides in 20 minutes] had a hybrid or intermediate density.

The text explains that the DNA-dependent DNA polymerases catalyse polymerisation only in one direction, that is 5' à3'.

The text explains that since the two strands have opposite polarity and the DNA-dependent RNA polymerase also catalyse the polymerisation in only one direction, that is, 5' →3' , the strand that has the polarity 3' →5' acts as a template, and is also referred to as template strand.

The text explains that this led to the proposition of a genetic code that could direct the sequence of amino acids during synthesis of proteins.

The text explains that for example, if an enzyme called beta-galactosidase is synthesised by E. coli, it is used to catalyse the hydrolysis of a disaccharide, lactose into galactose and glucose; the bacteria use them as a source of energy.

The text explains that hence, if the bacteria do not have lactose around them to be utilised for energy source, they would no longer require the synthesis of the enzyme beta-galactosidase.

The text explains that in the absence of a preferred carbon source such as glucose, if lactose is provided in the growth medium of the bacteria, the lactose is transported into the cells through the action of permease (Remember, a very low level of expression of lac operon has to be present in the cell all the time, otherwise lactose cannot enter the cells).

The text explains that they are thought to have no direct coding functions, but they shed light on chromosome structure, dynamics and evolution.

The text explains that these repetitive DNA are separated from bulk genomic DNA as different peaks during density gradient centrifugation.

The text explains that during transcription also, one of the strands of DNA acts a template to direct the synthesis of complementary RNA.

The text explains that in bacteria, the transcribed mRNA is functional, hence can directly be translated.

The text explains that if the sequence of one strand of DNA is written as follows: 5' -ATGCATGCATGCATGCATGCATGCATGC-3' Write down the sequence of complementary strand in 5' →3' direction.

The text explains that which property of DNA double helix led Watson and Crick to hypothesise semi-conservative mode of DNA replication?

The text explains that depending upon the chemical nature of the template (DNA or RNA) and the nature of nucleic acids synthesised from it (DNA or RNA), list the types of nucleic acid polymerases.

The text explains that how did Hershey and Chase differentiate between DNA and protein in their experiment while proving that DNA is the genetic material?

The text explains that differentiate between the followings: (a) Repetitive DNA and Satellite DNA (b) mRNA and tRNA (c) Template strand and Coding strand 9.

The text explains that list two essential roles of ribosome during translation.

The text explains that in the medium where E. coli was growing, lactose was added, which induced the lac operon.

The text explains that then, why does lac operon shut down some time after addition of lactose in the medium?

The text explains that explain (in one or two lines) the function of the followings: (a) Promoter (b) tRNA (c) Exons 12.

The text explains that why is the Human Genome project called a mega project?

The text explains that briefly describe the following: (a) Transcription (b) Polymorphism (c) Translation (d) Bioinformatics

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5: Principles of Inheritance

prelims_traps: Do not treat CHAPTER 5 and MOLECULAR BASIS OF as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Principles of Inheritance; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Principles of Inheritance through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use CHAPTER 5 and MOLECULAR BASIS OF to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
